resume_analysis.title: Brutally Honest Resume Analysis
strengths: React Native (Expo): Built 50+ user flows with hooks, navigation, auth — this is real, not tutorial-level
S3 Direct Upload: Architecting client-side multipart upload pipeline is genuinely good backend thinking
State machines & conversation flows: WhatsApp 15+ flows with agent escalation shows systems thinking
MongoDB ETL at scale: 25k+ records with dedup, hashing, batch inserts — actually solid data engineering
n8n workflow automation: Used AI tooling in production — niche but marketable
RBAC implementation: Not every fresher thinks about authorization properly
MCA from PES with 9.0 CGPA + Merit Scholarship: Academic pedigree checks out
roasts: You listed 'WebSockets' on your resume but your projects show ZERO real-time implementation. PharmaSync? Publications app? Where's the WebSocket? You're lying on your resume. Fix that or be ready to get destroyed in interviews.

Next.js is listed as a core skill but your 'Next.js projects' are just React apps with SSR sprinkled on. You don't understand ISR vs SSR vs SSG vs PPR. You can't talk about App Router internals. Any senior will sniff this out in 5 minutes.

Redis is on your resume. What have you actually used it for? Nothing in your projects mentions caching, pub/sub, rate limiting, or sessions via Redis. That's a resume lie. A lie that will humiliate you in interviews.
Docker: You listed it. One dockerized project? Compose? Multi-stage builds? Health checks? Nothing concrete visible. This is filler right now.
TypeScript: You list it but your React Native work likely has tons of any-typed code or you switched between JS and TS without real discipline. Your TS level is probably basic generics at best.
DSA is weak by your own admission. That means you cannot pass FAANG/top startup screening rounds. At your target salary jump, this is non-negotiable.
Zero open source contribution. Zero public project with stars. Zero technical blog. You exist only on paper. Employers can't verify you outside your resume.

System Design knowledge: Nowhere. No mention of any design pattern, CAP theorem, distributed systems, or architecture document. You're an MCA graduate — this should have been drilled in, but it clearly wasn't.

Performance Engineering: You claim to be interested in performance but there is NOT A SINGLE METRIC in your resume. No Lighthouse score. No bundle size reduction. No latency improvement numbers (except the S3 one). That's thin.

Your total work experience is about 8 months at ONE startup that shut down. Depending on the market, this can look risky. You need a GitHub profile that screams 'hire me' before a human even reads your resume.

reasons: You already know Python (listed on resume, used in ETL pipelines). Zero ramp-up cost.
LeetCode Python solutions are 40-60% shorter than C++ equivalents. Time is your constraint.
Python is fully accepted at all top Indian product companies (Zepto, Cred, Razorpay, Swiggy, etc.)
C++ gives microsecond advantages in competitive programming. In interviews, nobody cares — they care about correctness + logic.
With 60 days, spending 2+ weeks learning C++ syntax would be criminal waste.
dsa_language_decision.warning: You will write Python for ALL DSA. No debates. No switching. Consistency beats theoretical efficiency.
dsa_language_decision.tip: Use Python's collections (defaultdict, Counter, deque, heapq) aggressively. They are your competitive edge.

specialization_decision.reasoning: You are 8 months into your career. The job pool for full-stack is maximum at SDE-1 level in India. Full-stack lets you interview for frontend, backend, fullstack, AND platform roles simultaneously.
specialization_decision.goal: Be a DANGEROUS full-stack engineer who is scary good at Next.js and real-time systems. That is the profile that gets you 18-30 LPA roles at product companies.

days.title: TypeScript: The Real Foundation
days.afternoon_task: Build a TypeScript utility library — 5 functions using advanced types.

topics.title: Type System Fundamentals
topics.context: You listed TypeScript on resume. If you can't explain structural typing vs nominal typing you're done.
subtopics: Primitive types: string, number, boolean, null, undefined, symbol, bigint
Type vs Interface: when to use each, declaration merging (only interface does this)
Union types (string | number) vs Intersection types (TypeA & TypeB)
Literal types: const x: 'hello' = 'hello' — why this is powerful
Type narrowing: typeof, instanceof, in operator, discriminated unions
never type: exhaustive checks, unreachable code, function that throws or loops forever
unknown vs any: unknown forces type checking, any is a lie

topics.title: Advanced Types
subtopics: Generics: <T>, constraints with extends, multiple generics <T, K extends keyof T>
Utility types: Partial<T>, Required<T>, Readonly<T>, Pick<T,K>, Omit<T,K>, Record<K,V>
Mapped types: { [K in keyof T]: T[K] } — how they work under the hood
Conditional types: T extends string ? 'yes' : 'no' — practical uses
Infer keyword in conditional types — extracting types from function return values
Template literal types: `${string}Id` for typed string patterns

topics.title: TypeScript with React
subtopics: Typing React components: FC vs explicit return types (prefer explicit)
useState generics: useState<User | null>(null)
useRef typing: useRef<HTMLInputElement>(null)
Event typing: React.ChangeEvent<HTMLInputElement>, React.MouseEvent<HTMLButtonElement>
Custom hooks with return types
Typing context: createContext with default values

questions: What is the difference between interface and type alias? Give a concrete example of where they behave differently.
Explain discriminated unions. Write a Shape type with Circle, Rectangle, Triangle and a function that uses exhaustive checking with never.
What does the infer keyword do? Write a ReturnType<T> utility type from scratch without using the built-in.
What's wrong with using any? When would you legitimately use unknown instead?
Write a DeepReadonly<T> mapped type that recursively makes all nested properties readonly.
Explain how TypeScript's structural typing works. Can you assign { name: string, age: number } to { name: string }?
What is a conditional type? Write a NonNullable<T> implementation.
How do you type a function that accepts a key of an object and returns the value at that key?
What is the difference between Partial<T> and Required<T>? Write both from scratch.
Explain template literal types. How would you create a type EventName that is `on${Capitalize<string>}`?

days.title: TypeScript Advanced + Async Patterns
days.tip: Build a small typed Express app today: User CRUD with full TypeScript, no any, proper error types.

topics.title: TypeScript Async & Error Handling
subtopics: Typing async functions: Promise<User>, Promise<void>
Result pattern: type Result<T,E> = { ok: true; value: T } | { ok: false; error: E }
Never throw raw errors — always type your errors
Discriminated union error handling — real production pattern
Axios response typing, fetch with generics

topics.title: TypeScript Module System & Declaration Files
subtopics: Modules: ES modules vs CommonJS in TypeScript context
Declaration files (.d.ts): when to write them and why
Ambient declarations: declare module, declare global
@types packages: what they are, how DefinitelyTyped works
tsconfig.json deep dive: strict, target, lib, paths, baseUrl, moduleResolution
Path aliases in TypeScript (@/components → src/components)

topics.title: TypeScript with Node.js/Express
subtopics: Typing Express Request: extending Request interface with user property
Middleware typing with RequestHandler<Params, ResBody, ReqBody, Query>
Typed environment variables with a schema validation approach
Class decorators basics (important for NestJS if you go there)

How do you extend Express's Request interface to include a user property?

days.title: Next.js App Router: Deep Internals
days.roast: If you cannot explain the difference between ISR and PPR right now, your 'Next.js specialist' claim is a joke. This is the most important day of Week 1.

topics.title: Rendering Strategies — The Core
topics.context: This is your biggest gap. You need to understand this deeply enough to defend architecture decisions.
subtopics: SSR (Server-Side Rendering): Rendered on server per request. getServerSideProps (Pages Router). Async Server Component (App Router).
SSG (Static Site Generation): HTML built at COMPILE time. getStaticProps. generateStaticParams. Fastest possible delivery.
ISR (Incremental Static Regeneration): SSG + revalidation interval. revalidate property. Background regeneration.
CSR (Client-Side Rendering): 'use client' components. Data fetched in browser. Worst for SEO, best for interactivity.
PPR (Partial Pre-Rendering): Next.js 15+ feature. Static shell + dynamic holes with Suspense. The future.
On-demand revalidation: revalidatePath(), revalidateTag() — trigger ISR manually from API routes or server actions

topics.title: App Router Architecture (Your New Bible)
subtopics: File-based routing: page.tsx, layout.tsx, loading.tsx, error.tsx, not-found.tsx, route.ts
Layouts: root layout, nested layouts, parallel routes with @slot convention
Server Components vs Client Components: Server by default, 'use client' opts in to client
What CAN'T Server Components do: no useState, no useEffect, no browser APIs, no event handlers
What CAN Server Components do: async/await directly, access databases directly, keep secrets server-side
Data fetching in Server Components: fetch with {cache: 'no-store'} vs {next: {revalidate: 60}}
Server Actions: async functions with 'use server'. Form submissions without API routes.
Streaming with Suspense: <Suspense fallback={<Loading/>}> wrapping async server components
Route Groups: (marketing), (app) — organize routes without affecting URL structure
Dynamic routes: [id], [...slug], [[...slug]] — catch-all and optional catch-all
Intercepting routes: (.) same level, (..) one level up, (...) root — for modals pattern

topics.title: Next.js Image, Font, and Metadata APIs
subtopics: next/image: width/height required, fill prop, priority for LCP images, quality, formats
Automatic WebP/AVIF conversion, lazy loading by default, blur placeholder
next/font: zero layout shift, self-hosted, font-display swap automatically
Metadata API: generateMetadata(), static metadata object, opengraph, twitter cards
generateStaticParams(): pre-generating dynamic routes for SSG

questions: Explain ISR. What is the revalidate property and how does background regeneration work? What happens if a request comes in during regeneration?
What is Partial Pre-Rendering (PPR)? How does it differ from ISR? What version of Next.js introduced it?
In the App Router, what is the difference between a Server Component and a Client Component? List 5 things each can and cannot do.
Explain the 'waterfall' problem in data fetching. How does Suspense solve it? Write an example.
What is a Server Action? Write a form submission without an API route using Server Actions.
How does revalidatePath() work? When would you use it over setting a revalidate interval?
Explain parallel routes and intercepting routes. Give a real use case (modal on top of a page).
What is the difference between layout.tsx and template.tsx? When would you use template?
How does next/image optimize images under the hood? What formats does it serve and when?
What does the generateStaticParams function do? Write an example for a blog with dynamic [slug] routes.

days.title: Next.js Performance + Middleware + Edge
days.tip: Build: a Next.js app with middleware-based auth, ISR pages, and dynamic imports. Measure before and after Lighthouse scores.

topics.title: Next.js Performance Optimization
subtopics: Core Web Vitals: LCP (Largest Contentful Paint < 2.5s), FID/INP (< 200ms), CLS (< 0.1)
Bundle analysis: next build && next analyze — @next/bundle-analyzer
Code splitting: automatic per-page, dynamic() for component-level splitting
dynamic() with ssr: false — client-only components, no SSR for heavy libraries
Tree shaking: how Next.js eliminates dead code (ESM required)
Route prefetching: Link prefetch={true} (default) vs prefetch={false}
Font optimization: next/font, self-hosted, no external network requests
Script optimization: next/script with strategy: 'lazyOnload' | 'afterInteractive' | 'beforeInteractive'
Image optimization: priority prop for above-fold, sizes prop for responsive

topics.title: Caching Architecture in Next.js — All 4 Layers
subtopics: Request Memoization: same fetch URL in same render tree is called ONCE automatically
Data Cache: fetch results stored on server across requests (persistent). unstable_cache for non-fetch calls.
Full Route Cache: static HTML/RSC payload stored at build time or after first request (SSG/ISR)
Router Cache: client-side cache in browser for RSC payloads — navigated routes are cached
How to opt out: cache: 'no-store', no-cache in fetch options, dynamic = 'force-dynamic' in route
cache() function: memoize arbitrary async functions within a request

topics.title: Middleware
subtopics: middleware.ts: runs at the Edge before a request is processed
matcher config: which routes middleware applies to
Use cases: auth redirects, A/B testing, geo-redirects, rate limiting at edge, feature flags
NextRequest, NextResponse APIs
Edge Runtime vs Node.js Runtime: edge has limited APIs (no fs, no native Node modules)
Geolocation and IP headers available in middleware

questions: Explain Next.js's 4 caching layers. What is the difference between Data Cache and Full Route Cache?
What is Request Memoization? Does it persist across requests?
How do you force a Server Component to always run dynamically (never cache)?
What is the Edge Runtime? What Node.js APIs are NOT available on it?
Write a middleware.ts that redirects unauthenticated users to /login based on a JWT cookie.
What is the unstable_cache function? When do you need it vs fetch with cache options?
Explain code splitting in Next.js. What happens automatically vs what you need dynamic() for?
What are Core Web Vitals? What are the passing thresholds for LCP, INP, and CLS?
How does the Router Cache work? Can you programmatically invalidate it?

days.title: DSA Focus Day: Arrays, Strings & Sliding Window
days.context: DSA sessions happen EVERY morning (6-7:30am). Friday is your first full DSA focus day to consolidate the week.

topics.title: Array Fundamentals in Python
subtopics: list operations: append O(1) amortized, insert O(n), pop O(1) from end, pop(0) O(n)
Two pointers technique: left/right pointers moving toward each other
Prefix sums: precompute cumulative sums for range queries in O(1)
Kadane's algorithm: maximum subarray in O(n)
Dutch National Flag: sort 0s, 1s, 2s in O(n) single pass

topics.title: Sliding Window — HIGH Frequency Interview Pattern
subtopics: Fixed window: maintain sum/state for window of size k
Variable window: expand right, shrink left when condition violated
Pattern: two pointers + a frequency map (Counter) for string problems
Problems to solve today: Maximum subarray, Longest substring without repeating chars, Minimum window substring, Sliding window maximum

topics.title: String Manipulation
subtopics: String immutability in Python — always use list then join for O(n) builds
Anagram check: Counter(s) == Counter(t) or sorted comparison
Palindrome: two pointer from ends
KMP Algorithm: pattern matching in O(n+m) — understand the failure function

days.title: Node.js Internals + Event Loop Mastery
days.roast: You built an API with Node.js at Defyd. Can you explain what happens in the event loop when your route handler makes a MongoDB query? If you can't answer that, you don't understand Node.js, you just used it.

topics.title: The Event Loop — Most Asked Node.js Interview Topic
subtopics: Single-threaded but non-blocking: how Node handles concurrency
Event Loop phases in ORDER: timers, pending callbacks, idle/prepare, poll, check, close callbacks
Timers phase: setTimeout, setInterval callbacks
Poll phase: I/O callbacks, the loop blocks here waiting for events
Check phase: setImmediate callbacks run here
Microtasks: process.nextTick (BEFORE next phase) and Promise.then (after nextTick, before next phase)
ORDER of execution: sync code → process.nextTick → Promise.then → setTimeout/setImmediate
Thread pool (libuv): file I/O, DNS, crypto operations run on 4 threads by default. UV_THREADPOOL_SIZE env.

topics.title: Streams — Underrated but Critical
subtopics: 4 types: Readable, Writable, Duplex, Transform
Streaming vs buffering: why streaming a 5GB file works, buffering crashes the process
pipe() and pipeline(): the right way to chain streams, pipeline handles errors
Backpressure: when writable can't keep up with readable — highWaterMark
Real use case: streaming file upload to S3, streaming CSV parsing, HTTP response streaming
stream.pipeline with async generators — modern pattern

topics.title: Node.js Cluster & Worker Threads
subtopics: Cluster module: spawn N worker processes for CPU core utilization
Master-worker model: master manages, workers share same port via OS round-robin
Worker Threads: true parallelism for CPU-intensive work (NOT I/O)
SharedArrayBuffer: share memory between worker threads
When to use cluster vs worker threads vs just async I/O

questions: List all phases of the Node.js event loop in order. What happens in the poll phase?
What is the difference between process.nextTick and setImmediate? Which runs first?
What is backpressure in streams? How does highWaterMark relate to it?
Explain the difference between Worker Threads and Cluster. When would you use each?

What is the output order? console.log(1); setTimeout(()=>console.log(2),0); Promise.resolve().then(()=>console.log(3)); console.log(4);
How does Node.js handle 10,000 concurrent HTTP requests with a single thread?
What is stream.pipeline and why is it preferred over .pipe()?
How would you stream a large CSV file through Node.js to a client without running out of memory?
What is the difference between Readable in flowing vs paused mode?

topics.title: Query Optimization & Indexes
subtopics: B-Tree index: default, ideal for equality and range queries, ordered
Hash index: equality only, faster for exact match, not range
GIN index: for JSONB, arrays, full-text search
GiST index: for geometric types, full-text, nearest-neighbor
Partial indexes: CREATE INDEX ON users(email) WHERE deleted_at IS NULL — smaller, faster
Composite indexes: order matters — (last_name, first_name) serves queries on last_name but not first_name alone
Index-only scans: when all needed columns are in the index (covering indexes)
EXPLAIN ANALYZE: reading query plans, understanding Seq Scan vs Index Scan vs Index Only Scan
seq_page_cost vs random_page_cost: why planner sometimes prefers seq scan

topics.title: Advanced SQL Patterns
subtopics: Window functions: ROW_NUMBER(), RANK(), DENSE_RANK(), LAG(), LEAD(), FIRST_VALUE()
PARTITION BY with window functions: per-group calculations
CTEs (WITH clause): readable complex queries, recursive CTEs for hierarchical data
DISTINCT ON: PostgreSQL-specific, get first row per group efficiently
Upsert: INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE SET ... — atomic, no race conditions
JSONB operators: ->, ->>, @>, ?, #>, jsonb_set(), jsonb_build_object()
Full-text search: to_tsvector, to_tsquery, @@ operator, ts_rank

topics.title: Transactions & Isolation
subtopics: ACID properties: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability — what each means concretely
Isolation levels: READ UNCOMMITTED, READ COMMITTED (PG default), REPEATABLE READ, SERIALIZABLE
Dirty reads, non-repeatable reads, phantom reads: which level prevents which
Optimistic vs pessimistic locking: SELECT FOR UPDATE (pessimistic), version column (optimistic)
Deadlocks: what causes them, how PostgreSQL detects and resolves, how to prevent
Long transactions: why they are dangerous (hold locks, block autovacuum, bloat WAL)

questions: What is the difference between B-Tree and Hash indexes in PostgreSQL? When would you use each?

days.title: Redis: Make That Resume Lie True
days.tip: Build today: Express API with Redis caching (cache-aside), rate limiting middleware using sliding window, and a simple pub/sub notification system.

topics.title: Redis Data Structures & Use Cases
subtopics: String: simple key-value, counters (INCR), rate limiting, session store, feature flags
Hash: store object fields, HSET user:1 name 'Raghav' age '25', HGETALL — like a row in memory
List: LPUSH/RPUSH, LPOP/RPOP, LRANGE — message queues, activity feeds, recent items
Set: SADD, SMEMBERS, SINTER (intersection), SUNION, SDIFF — unique items, tagging
Sorted Set (ZSet): ZADD with score, ZRANGEBYSCORE, ZREVRANK — leaderboards, priority queues, time-series
HyperLogLog: approximate unique count with tiny memory — COUNT DISTINCT at scale
Pub/Sub: PUBLISH channel message, SUBSCRIBE channel — real-time messaging
Streams: XADD, XREAD, consumer groups — Kafka-lite, audit logs, event sourcing

topics.title: Redis Caching Patterns
subtopics: Cache-aside (lazy loading): app checks cache first, on miss reads DB and populates cache
Write-through: write to cache AND DB simultaneously — always fresh, but write latency
Write-behind (write-back): write to cache, async flush to DB — risky, fastest writes
TTL strategy: EXPIRE, EXPIREAT, PEXPIRE (milliseconds)
Cache stampede / thundering herd: many requests hit DB on cache miss simultaneously
Solution: probabilistic early expiration, locking with SETNX (SET if Not eXists)
Cache invalidation: hardest problem. Tag-based invalidation with Redis Sets.

topics.title: Redis Rate Limiting Patterns
subtopics: Fixed window counter: INCR key, EXPIRE on first INCR — simple but boundary burst issue
Sliding window log: ZADD with timestamp score, ZREMRANGEBYSCORE — accurate but memory heavy
Sliding window counter: two windows with weighted average — balance of accuracy and memory
Token bucket: more complex but smoother rate limiting
Implementing with Lua scripts for atomicity: redis.call inside EVAL

topics.title: Redis Pub/Sub vs Streams
subtopics: Pub/Sub: fire and forget, no persistence, subscriber must be online. Good for real-time events.
Streams: persistent log, consumer groups, acknowledgment, replay. Good for reliable messaging.
When to use Pub/Sub: live notifications, chat
When to use Streams: event sourcing, audit log, reliable job queues

questions: What Redis data structure would you use for a leaderboard? How would you get the top 10 users?
Explain cache-aside pattern. Write the code flow step by step.
What is a cache stampede? How do you prevent it with SETNX?
What is the difference between Redis Pub/Sub and Streams? When do you choose Streams?
Implement a fixed window rate limiter in Redis using INCR and EXPIRE. What is the boundary burst problem?
What is HyperLogLog? When would you use it over a Set?
How would you implement distributed session management with Redis?
What is a Redis pipeline? How does it differ from a transaction (MULTI/EXEC)?
Explain Redis eviction policies. What is LRU and LFU?
How do you implement a sliding window rate limiter using a Sorted Set?

days.title: WebSockets & Real-Time Systems (The Big Fix)
days.roast: You listed WebSockets on your resume and your projects have ZERO real-time. Today you fix that and build something real. This is not optional.
days.tip: Build: A real-time collaborative document editor or live chat with Socket.IO, Redis adapter, rooms, and typing indicators. Push to GitHub TODAY.

topics.title: WebSocket Protocol Deep Dive
subtopics: HTTP vs WebSocket: HTTP is request-response, WS is persistent bidirectional TCP connection
WebSocket handshake: HTTP Upgrade request → 101 Switching Protocols response
Frames: text frames, binary frames, ping/pong frames, close frames
WS URL: ws:// (plaintext) and wss:// (TLS encrypted)
Browser WebSocket API: new WebSocket(url), onopen, onmessage, onerror, onclose, send()
ReadyState: CONNECTING(0), OPEN(1), CLOSING(2), CLOSED(3)

topics.title: Socket.IO — Industry Standard
subtopics: Socket.IO over raw WebSocket: automatic fallback to long-polling, reconnection, rooms, namespaces
Rooms: socket.join(room), io.to(room).emit() — group messaging
Namespaces: /chat, /admin — separate concerns on same connection
Events: socket.on(), socket.emit(), io.emit() (broadcast all), socket.broadcast.emit() (all except sender)
Acknowledgments: emit with callback for guaranteed delivery
Middleware in Socket.IO: authentication, logging
socket.io-adapter with Redis adapter: horizontal scaling across multiple Node.js processes

topics.title: Scaling Real-Time Systems
subtopics: Sticky sessions: route same client to same server (needed if no Redis adapter)
Redis Pub/Sub adapter: broadcast events across multiple server instances
Redis Streams for message persistence: replay missed events for reconnected clients
Load balancing WebSockets: L4 load balancer (TCP passthrough) vs L7 (HTTP upgrade aware)
Connection management: heartbeat/ping-pong to detect dead connections
Rate limiting on WebSocket messages: prevent abuse

topics.title: Server-Sent Events (SSE) — Know the Alternative
subtopics: SSE: one-directional server→client over HTTP/1.1. EventSource API in browser.
When to use SSE over WebSocket: notifications, live feed, progress updates (server→client only)
SSE advantages: works over HTTP/2, proxies handle it, auto-reconnect built-in
SSE in Next.js App Router: streaming with ReadableStream from route handlers

questions: Explain the WebSocket handshake process. What HTTP headers are involved?
What is the difference between socket.broadcast.emit() and io.emit()?
How do you scale Socket.IO across multiple Node.js instances? What does the Redis adapter do?
When would you use SSE instead of WebSocket? Give 2 real examples.
What is a Socket.IO room? Write code to implement a private chat between two users.
How do you authenticate WebSocket connections? Show the Socket.IO middleware pattern.
What happens when a WebSocket connection drops? How does Socket.IO handle reconnection?
How do you implement typing indicators in a chat app with Socket.IO?
What is the sticky session problem? How does the Redis adapter solve it?
Explain ping/pong frames. Why are they important for connection health?

days.title: API Design: REST Mastery + GraphQL Intro

topics.title: REST API Design Best Practices
subtopics: Resource naming: nouns not verbs. /users not /getUsers. /orders/123/items not /getOrderItems
HTTP methods semantics: GET (idempotent, safe), POST (create, not idempotent), PUT (full replace, idempotent), PATCH (partial update), DELETE (idempotent)

Status codes: 200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content, 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found, 409 Conflict, 422 Unprocessable Entity, 429 Too Many Requests, 500 Internal Server Error
Filtering, sorting, pagination: ?page=1&limit=20&sort=createdAt:desc&filter[status]=active
Cursor-based pagination vs offset: cursor is faster for large datasets (no COUNT(*)), offset has page jump capability
Versioning strategies: URL versioning (/v1/users), header versioning (Accept: application/vnd.api.v1+json)
HATEOAS: responses include links to related actions — more self-describing APIs
Rate limiting headers: X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset, Retry-After

topics.title: API Security
subtopics: JWT deep dive: header.payload.signature, HS256 vs RS256, token expiry, refresh token pattern
Refresh token rotation: issue new refresh token on use, invalidate old one — stateless revocation
OAuth 2.0 flows: Authorization Code (web apps), PKCE (SPAs), Client Credentials (M2M)
API Key authentication: hashed storage, scoped permissions, key rotation
Request validation: zod/joi schema validation before business logic
SQL injection prevention: parameterized queries ALWAYS, never string concat
CORS configuration: origins whitelist, credentials: true implications
Helmet.js: security headers — CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options, etc.

topics.title: GraphQL Fundamentals (Awareness Level)
subtopics: Schema definition: type Query, type Mutation, type Subscription
Resolvers: function that returns data for a field
N+1 problem: naive resolvers make N DB calls for N items — solved by DataLoader (batching)
GraphQL vs REST: GraphQL wins on flexibility/overfetch, REST wins on caching/simplicity
When companies use GraphQL: when clients have wildly different data needs (mobile vs web)

questions: Design a REST API for a food delivery app. List the endpoints for orders with proper HTTP methods and status codes.
What is cursor-based pagination? When is it better than offset pagination?
Explain JWT structure. How do you invalidate a JWT token?
What is refresh token rotation? Why is it more secure than long-lived JWTs?
What is the N+1 problem in GraphQL? How does DataLoader solve it?
What is the difference between 401 and 403 status codes?
How do you implement idempotency in a payment API? What is an idempotency key?
What headers should your API return for rate limiting? What should the client do with Retry-After?
Explain OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code flow with PKCE. Why is PKCE needed for SPAs?
What is HATEOAS? Give an example of a HATEOAS-style response.

topics.title: Linked Lists
subtopics: Singly vs Doubly linked list: node structure, pointer manipulation
Dummy/sentinel node pattern: simplifies edge cases at head/tail
Floyd's cycle detection: slow/fast pointers — O(1) space cycle detection
Reverse a linked list: iterative (3 pointers) and recursive
Merge two sorted lists, find middle node (slow/fast pointer), detect cycle, remove nth from end

topics.title: Stacks & Queues
subtopics: Stack: LIFO. Python: list with append/pop. Use cases: balanced parentheses, function call stack, undo/redo
Monotonic stack: maintain increasing or decreasing order for next greater/smaller element problems
Queue: FIFO. Python: collections.deque with append/popleft (O(1)). NOT list.pop(0) which is O(n)
Monotonic deque: sliding window maximum problem
BFS uses a queue. DFS uses a stack (or recursion).

leetcode_problems.title: Remove Nth Node From End of List

questions: Write Floyd's cycle detection algorithm. How does it work to find the cycle start?
What is a monotonic stack? How does it solve the 'Next Greater Element' problem?
Why should you use collections.deque for a queue instead of a Python list?
Write a function to reverse a linked list iteratively with O(1) space.
What is the dummy node pattern? Show how it simplifies removing the head of a linked list.
How do you find the middle of a linked list in one pass?
Write an LRU Cache using an OrderedDict or doubly linked list + hash map.
Explain the sliding window maximum problem. Why is a monotonic deque O(n)?
What is the difference between a stack and a call stack?
Implement a stack that supports push, pop, and getMin in O(1) each.

topics.title: React Rendering Mechanics
subtopics: Why React re-renders: state change, prop change, context value change, parent re-render
Reconciliation: React compares VDOM trees, patches DOM minimally
Key prop: signals React which list items are same/new. Keys must be stable, unique, and NOT index in reordered lists.
Fiber architecture: Fiber is a unit of work. React can pause, resume, prioritize rendering.
Concurrent features: useTransition (mark updates as non-urgent), useDeferredValue (defer expensive re-renders)

topics.title: Memoization APIs
subtopics: React.memo: memoizes a component, skips re-render if props unchanged (shallow compare)
useMemo: memoizes a computed VALUE. const value = useMemo(() => expensive(a,b), [a,b])
useCallback: memoizes a FUNCTION reference. const fn = useCallback(() => {}, [deps])
When NOT to use memo: premature optimization. Memo adds cost. Only memoize proven bottlenecks.
Referential equality trap: objects and arrays are new on every render. useMemo fixes this.
Profiler API: React DevTools Profiler — measure which components are slow and why

topics.title: State Management Performance
subtopics: Context performance: every context consumer re-renders when context VALUE changes. Split contexts.
Zustand: lightweight, no boilerplate, selective subscriptions (only re-render on the slice you subscribe to)
Jotai: atomic state, derive state from atoms
React Query / TanStack Query: server state management. Caching, background refetch, optimistic updates.
Why not Redux for everything: too much boilerplate for most apps. Use Zustand for client state + React Query for server state.

topics.title: Code Splitting & Lazy Loading
subtopics: React.lazy + Suspense: dynamic import of component, show fallback while loading
Route-level code splitting: import Dashboard when /dashboard route is hit
Preloading: import() call in mouseenter/touchstart to preload before click

questions: What triggers a React re-render? List all possible causes.
What is the difference between useMemo and useCallback? Give a concrete example of each.
When would you NOT use React.memo? What are the costs?
Explain React Fiber. What problem does it solve compared to the old stack reconciler?
What is the 'context performance problem'? How do you solve it?
What is useTransition? Build an example: a search filter that uses useTransition.
How does React Query handle caching? What is staleTime vs gcTime?
Why is key={index} bad for lists that can be reordered or filtered?
Explain optimistic updates in React Query. What happens if the mutation fails?
What does React DevTools Profiler measure? What are you looking for in a flame chart?

days.title: Week 2 Review + System Design Intro + DSA: Trees

topics.title: System Design Introduction
subtopics: What interviewers want: a structured approach, not the right answer
Framework: Clarify requirements → Estimate scale → API design → Database design → High-level design → Deep dive → Bottlenecks
CAP Theorem: Consistency, Availability, Partition Tolerance — pick 2. Real systems make tradeoffs.
Horizontal vs vertical scaling: scale out (more machines) vs scale up (bigger machine)
Load balancer: distributes traffic, health checks, algorithms (round robin, least connections, IP hash)
Database: read replicas for read scaling, sharding for write scaling
CDN: static asset delivery from edge nodes close to users

topics.title: DSA: Binary Trees & BST
subtopics: Tree traversals: inorder (left-root-right), preorder (root-left-right), postorder (left-right-root) — both iterative and recursive
BFS (level order): use a queue, process level by level
DFS patterns: most tree problems are DFS with a choice of what to return or pass down
BST property: left subtree < root, right subtree > root. Inorder traversal of BST = sorted array.
Common patterns: find height, check balance, find LCA, validate BST, kth smallest

questions: Write iterative inorder traversal of a binary tree without recursion.
What is the CAP theorem? Give an example of a CP system and an AP system.
How does a load balancer decide which server to route a request to? List 3 algorithms.
What is database sharding? What are the challenges of sharding?
How do you find the Lowest Common Ancestor of two nodes in a Binary Tree (not BST)?
Validate if a binary tree is a valid BST. What is the elegant approach using min/max bounds?
Explain the difference between read replicas and sharding. When do you use each?
What is a CDN? How does cache invalidation work in a CDN?
Write BFS level order traversal and return results as a list of lists (one per level).
What is consistent hashing? Why is it used for distributing cache across multiple nodes?

days.title: Docker: From Filler to Real Skill
days.tip: Build: Dockerize your Node.js + PostgreSQL + Redis app with docker-compose. Multi-stage Dockerfile for Node. Healthchecks on DB. Hot reload in development with bind mount.

topics.title: Docker Fundamentals — Deep
subtopics: Container vs VM: containers share host OS kernel, VMs have separate OS. Containers are lighter.
Dockerfile instructions: FROM, WORKDIR, COPY, RUN, ENV, EXPOSE, CMD, ENTRYPOINT
CMD vs ENTRYPOINT: CMD is default args (overridable), ENTRYPOINT is fixed command
COPY vs ADD: COPY is explicit, ADD can extract archives and fetch URLs — prefer COPY
Layer caching: each instruction is a layer. Order matters — put infrequently changing things first
Build context: what gets sent to Docker daemon. Use .dockerignore to exclude node_modules, .git

topics.title: Multi-Stage Builds — Must Know
subtopics: Problem: dev dependencies, build tools bloat the final image
Solution: stage 1 builds, stage 2 copies only production artifacts
Example: FROM node:18 AS builder → npm ci, npm run build → FROM node:18-alpine → COPY --from=builder /app/dist
Result: image goes from 800MB to 150MB. This is what you mention in interviews.
Alpine images: minimal Linux, but missing some GNU tools. Prefer slim or alpine for production.

topics.title: Docker Compose
subtopics: docker-compose.yml: define services, networks, volumes
depends_on: start order (but not readiness — use healthcheck for readiness)
Environment variables: .env file, env_file option, environment block
Volumes: named volumes (persist across container restarts), bind mounts (for development hot reload)
Networks: services on same network can reach each other by service name (internal DNS)
healthcheck: liveness probe — when is container 'ready' vs just 'running'

topics.title: Docker Networking
subtopics: Bridge network: default, containers can talk to each other by container name
Host network: container uses host networking stack (no isolation, max performance, Linux only)
Overlay network: cross-host networking (Docker Swarm / Kubernetes context)
Port binding: -p 3000:3000 (host:container). EXPOSE is documentation only, not port binding.

questions: What is the difference between COPY and ADD in a Dockerfile?
Explain Docker layer caching. Why is the order of instructions important for caching?
Write a multi-stage Dockerfile for a Next.js app. What is the benefit?
What is the difference between a named volume and a bind mount?
How do services in docker-compose communicate with each other?
What does EXPOSE do in a Dockerfile? Does it actually bind the port?
What is the difference between CMD and ENTRYPOINT?
How do you add a healthcheck to a docker-compose service? Why is depends_on not enough?
What is .dockerignore? What should always be in it for a Node.js project?
How do you reduce a Docker image size? List 4 concrete techniques.

topics.title: Design a URL Shortener
subtopics: Functional requirements: create short URL, redirect, analytics
Scale estimate: 100M URLs/month write, 10B redirects/month read (100:1 read:write)
Core challenge: generate unique short codes — base62 encoding of auto-increment ID vs random
Database choice: NoSQL (DynamoDB/MongoDB) — simple key-value, horizontal scale
Cache (Redis): store {shortCode → longURL}. 80% traffic from 20% of URLs (hot URLs).
Redirect: 301 (permanent, browser caches — no analytics tracking) vs 302 (temporary, always hits server — better for analytics)
Custom aliases, expiry, analytics counters with Redis INCR

topics.title: Design a Notification System
subtopics: Types: push (mobile), email, SMS, in-app
Flow: API receives notification request → queue (Kafka/SQS) → workers per channel → delivery
Why queue: decouple producer from consumer, handle spikes, retry on failure
Priorities: transactional (OTP, password reset) must be fast, marketing can be batch
Fan-out: sending to 1M users. Need parallel workers, rate limits per channel provider.
Retry with exponential backoff: 1s, 2s, 4s, 8s... with jitter
Deduplication: idempotency key prevents sending same notification twice
Preferences: user opt-out, quiet hours, channel preferences stored in DB/cache

topics.title: Design a Rate Limiter
subtopics: Where: API gateway (centralized), middleware per service (distributed needs Redis)
Algorithms: fixed window, sliding window log, sliding window counter, token bucket, leaky bucket
Distributed rate limiting: Redis INCR with TTL for fixed window, ZSet for sliding window
Response: 429 Too Many Requests with Retry-After header
Different limits for different users: free tier 100/hour, premium 1000/hour

questions: Design a URL shortener. Walk through your full architecture: API, DB schema, caching, redirect mechanism.
What is the difference between 301 and 302 redirect in a URL shortener? Which tracks analytics better?
Why use a message queue for a notification system? What problem does it solve?
Explain exponential backoff with jitter. Why is jitter important?
Design the database schema for a notification system supporting email, SMS, and push.
How do you implement a token bucket rate limiter? What is the difference from a leaky bucket?
How does fan-out work for sending notifications to 1 million users?
What is a message queue dead letter queue (DLQ)? When should messages go there?
How do you prevent a notification from being sent twice (deduplication)?
How would you handle user time zones and quiet hours in a notification system?

days.title: Advanced Express: Middleware, Error Handling, Architecture

topics.title: Express Middleware Architecture
subtopics: Middleware signature: (req, res, next) => void. Error middleware: (err, req, res, next).
Order matters: middleware runs in order of registration
Application-level vs Router-level middleware
Building your own middleware: request logger, response time, correlation ID
Third-party must-haves: helmet (security), morgan (logging), compression, express-rate-limit

topics.title: Error Handling Properly
subtopics: NEVER throw in async route handlers without try-catch — unhandled rejection crashes Node
express-async-handler wrapper OR top-level async express support (Express 5)
Centralized error handler: 4-argument middleware at the END of your app
Custom AppError class: extends Error with statusCode and isOperational flag
Operational vs programmer errors: log differently, respond differently
Joi/Zod validation errors → 400, auth errors → 401, not found → 404, everything else → 500

topics.title: Project Architecture (MVC + Service Layer)
subtopics: Routes → Controller → Service → Repository → Database
Routes: URL matching only, no business logic
Controller: HTTP layer — parse req, call service, format res
Service: business logic, orchestration, no HTTP knowledge
Repository: database queries, no business logic
Why layers: testability (mock repository for service tests), replaceability
Dependency injection: pass dependencies as constructor args instead of importing directly

questions: Write a centralized error handling middleware for Express. What is the 4-argument signature?
What is the difference between operational and programmer errors? How do you handle them differently?
What does express-async-handler do and why is it needed in Express 4?
Design the folder structure for a production Express app with proper separation of concerns.
Write a correlation ID middleware that adds a unique ID to every request for tracing.
What is the Repository pattern? How does it make your service layer testable?
How does Zod validation work in an Express middleware? Show the pattern.
What is dependency injection? How does it help with testing?
Why must error-handling middleware be registered LAST in Express?
How do you gracefully shutdown an Express server on SIGTERM?

days.title: Testing: Jest/Vitest — Make Your Tests Actually Good

topics.title: Testing Philosophy & Types
subtopics: Unit tests: test one function/module in isolation. Mock all dependencies.
Integration tests: test multiple units together. Real DB (test DB) or in-memory.
E2E tests: full user flow in a browser. Playwright, Cypress.
Testing pyramid: many unit, fewer integration, few E2E. Inverse pyramid is expensive.
TDD: write test first, then code. Not always practical but shapes API design.
Coverage: aim for 80%+ on business logic. 100% is often not worth it.

topics.title: Jest/Vitest Patterns
subtopics: describe, it/test, expect, beforeEach, afterEach, beforeAll, afterAll
Matchers: toBe (strict equal), toEqual (deep equal), toContain, toThrow, toHaveBeenCalledWith
Mocking: jest.mock() for modules, jest.fn() for functions, jest.spyOn() for methods
Mock implementation: mockReturnValue, mockResolvedValue, mockRejectedValue
Async testing: async/await in test, resolves/rejects matchers
Testing Express: supertest library — make HTTP requests to your app in tests

topics.title: Testing React
subtopics: React Testing Library: test as a user would. findByText, getByRole, userEvent.
Avoid testing implementation details: don't test internal state, test what the user sees
Mock API calls: msw (Mock Service Worker) — intercept at network level
Testing hooks: @testing-library/react-hooks or renderHook

questions: What is the difference between toBe and toEqual in Jest?
How do you mock a module in Jest? Show the pattern for mocking a database repository.
Write a unit test for a service function that creates a user, with the repository mocked.
What is supertest? Write an integration test for a POST /users Express route.
What is Mock Service Worker (msw)? How does it differ from mocking fetch directly?
What is test coverage? Is 100% coverage always the goal?
Explain the difference between beforeEach and beforeAll. When do you use each?
How do you test a React component that fetches data? What do you mock?
What does jest.spyOn do differently from jest.fn()?
What makes a good unit test? List 5 properties.

topics.title: Graph Fundamentals
subtopics: Representations: adjacency list (most common — O(V+E) space), adjacency matrix (O(V²) — for dense graphs)
Directed vs undirected, weighted vs unweighted
Python graph as defaultdict(list)

topics.title: BFS on Graphs
subtopics: BFS: queue, visited set, level-by-level exploration
Use cases: shortest path in unweighted graph, level order, minimum steps
LC 200: Number of Islands (BFS flood fill)
LC 127: Word Ladder (BFS shortest transformation)
LC 286: Walls and Gates (multi-source BFS)

topics.title: DFS on Graphs
subtopics: DFS: recursive or iterative with stack, visited set to prevent cycles
Topological sort: DFS post-order OR Kahn's algorithm (BFS with in-degree)
Cycle detection: in directed graph — DFS with recursion stack, not just visited
Connected components, strongly connected components
LC 207: Course Schedule (topological sort / cycle detection)
LC 130: Surrounded Regions (DFS from border)

questions: What is the time complexity of BFS and DFS on a graph? What determines it?
How do you detect a cycle in a directed graph using DFS?
What is topological sort? When can't you topological sort a graph?
Explain Kahn's algorithm for topological sort using in-degree.

What data structure do you use for graph BFS in Python and why?

days.title: AWS Deep Dive: S3, IAM, EC2, Cognito

topics.title: S3 Mastery (You've Used It — Now Master It)
subtopics: Buckets: globally unique names, region-specific. Objects: key (path), value (bytes), metadata.
Presigned URLs: generate time-limited URLs for private objects. Clients upload/download directly.
Multipart upload: split large files, parallel upload, assemble on S3. You've done this — know the API.
S3 storage classes: Standard, Standard-IA (infrequent access), Glacier, Intelligent-Tiering
Lifecycle policies: automatically transition or delete objects based on age
Bucket policies vs ACLs: prefer bucket policies, ACLs are legacy
CORS configuration on S3: required for browser direct uploads
S3 versioning: keep multiple versions of same object — accidental delete protection
S3 Transfer Acceleration: CloudFront edge for faster upload over long distance

topics.title: IAM Deep Dive
subtopics: IAM Policies: JSON documents. Effect, Action, Resource, Condition.
IAM Roles vs Users: users have long-term credentials, roles are assumed temporarily
Principle of least privilege: grant ONLY what is needed
Instance profiles: EC2 instances assume roles — no hardcoded credentials
Cross-account access with roles
STS (Security Token Service): temporary credentials for assumed roles

topics.title: EC2 Basics + Cognito
subtopics: EC2 instance types: compute optimized (C), memory optimized (R), general purpose (T/M)
Security groups: stateful firewall. Inbound + outbound rules.
User Data: bootstrap script that runs on first launch
AMI: machine image — snapshot to launch EC2 from
AWS Cognito (you've used it): User Pools (authentication), Identity Pools (federated identities)
Cognito User Pool: handles sign-up, sign-in, MFA, JWT tokens. Hosted UI or custom UI.

questions: How do presigned S3 URLs work? What information is embedded in them?
Explain S3 multipart upload. At what file size should you switch from single to multipart?
What is the difference between an IAM Role and an IAM User?
What is an EC2 Instance Profile? Why should you use it instead of access keys on EC2?

How do you restrict an S3 bucket to only allow uploads from your application server?

days.title: Week 3 Review + Mock System Design Interview

schedule.task: 3 DSA problems — review weakest area from the week
Full mock system design session. Design a Twitter/X-like feed system. Time it. Draw it out.
Review Docker Compose setup, make sure all services run with one command
Write integration tests for your Express API using supertest
Read about message queues — RabbitMQ vs Kafka basics (awareness)
GitHub cleanup, README updates, commit streak check

topics.title: Compound Components
subtopics: Pattern: parent component shares state implicitly with children via Context
Example: <Select> + <Select.Option> — children access Select's state without explicit props
Implementation: createContext inside parent, children consume it
When to use: component libraries, flexible APIs with many variants

topics.title: Render Props & Children as Function
subtopics: Pattern: component accepts a function as children or prop, calls it with data
Example: <DataFetcher render={data => <List data={data}/>}/>
Modern alternative: custom hooks (cleaner, but render props still appear in interviews)

topics.title: HOC (Higher Order Components)
subtopics: Function that takes a component and returns enhanced component
Use case: cross-cutting concerns — auth guards, analytics, logging
Pitfalls: prop drilling complexity, displayName for debugging
Modern replacement: hooks + composition (but understand HOC for legacy code)

topics.title: Custom Hooks Patterns
subtopics: Encapsulate stateful logic, reuse across components
useDebounce: delay state updates for search input
usePrevious: ref to hold previous value
useIntersectionObserver: infinite scroll, lazy loading detection
useLocalStorage: sync state with localStorage
Rule: hooks must start with 'use', cannot be called conditionally

topics.title: Portals & Error Boundaries
subtopics: Portal: render child into DOM node outside parent hierarchy. Use case: modals, tooltips.
Error Boundary: class component (no hook equivalent yet) that catches render errors
Combine: portal-rendered modal + error boundary for resilient modals

questions: Implement a compound component pattern for a Tabs component with Tab.Header and Tab.Content.

What are the rules of hooks? Why do these rules exist?

topics.title: Next.js Build & Output
subtopics: next build: creates .next folder with optimized build. Shows page-by-page rendering strategy.
next start: production server (Node.js). Requires server for SSR/ISR.
output: 'export': generates static HTML. No server, no ISR, no dynamic routes without generateStaticParams.
Standalone output: output: 'standalone' in next.config — minimal production bundle for Docker
Environment variables: NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefix for client-side exposure. Server-only: access normally.

topics.title: Deploying on Vercel
subtopics: Zero-config deployment for Next.js. Git push → auto deploy.
Vercel Edge Functions: middleware runs on edge, near users globally
Vercel KV (Redis), Vercel Blob, Vercel Postgres — managed services
Preview deployments: every PR gets its own URL — great for team review
Environment variables: set per environment (development, preview, production)

topics.title: CI/CD with GitHub Actions
subtopics: Workflow: .github/workflows/ci.yml
Trigger: on push/PR to main
Steps: checkout → install deps → lint → type-check → test → build → deploy
Secrets: store API keys in GitHub Secrets, access as ${{ secrets.API_KEY }}
Caching: cache node_modules based on package-lock.json hash — faster CI
Matrix builds: test on Node 18 and 20 simultaneously

topics.title: Monitoring & Observability
subtopics: Three pillars: Logs (what happened), Metrics (how much), Traces (where time was spent)
Logging: structured JSON logs with winston or pino. Include: timestamp, level, message, requestId, userId.
Error tracking: Sentry — capture unhandled errors with stack traces, sourcemaps, context
APM: application performance monitoring. Response times, slow queries, throughput.
Health checks: /health endpoint returning DB/Redis connectivity status
Alerting: when error rate spikes or latency exceeds threshold

days.title: Database Design: Modeling Like a Senior

topics.title: Relational Database Design Principles
subtopics: Normalization: 1NF (atomic values), 2NF (no partial dependencies), 3NF (no transitive dependencies)
Denormalization: intentionally duplicate data for read performance (when reads >> writes)
Foreign keys: referential integrity, ON DELETE CASCADE vs RESTRICT vs SET NULL
Many-to-many: junction/pivot table with composite primary key
Soft deletes: deleted_at timestamp instead of actually deleting rows

topics.title: MongoDB Advanced Patterns
subtopics: Embedding vs referencing: embed when data is accessed together and small. Reference when data is large or shared.
Aggregation pipeline: $match → $group → $project → $sort → $limit

Transactions: multi-document ACID transactions (available in replica sets)
Schema validation: Mongoose validation vs MongoDB JSON Schema validator
Change streams: real-time data change notifications — pub/sub on DB events

topics.title: Database Connection Management
subtopics: Connection pooling: reuse connections instead of creating new one per request
Pool size: max connections = (CPU cores * 2) + disk spindles — rule of thumb
pg (node-postgres) pool: Pool constructor with max, idleTimeoutMillis, connectionTimeoutMillis
Mongoose connection: singleton pattern, retry logic on disconnect

days.title: DSA: Dynamic Programming — The Dragon
days.roast: Most developers memorize DP solutions without understanding the pattern. That's why they can't solve new DP problems in interviews. Today you learn the thinking, not just the solutions.

topics.title: DP Thinking Framework — 5 Steps
subtopics: Step 1: Identify if it's DP — optimal substructure + overlapping subproblems
Step 2: Define the state — what does dp[i] or dp[i][j] represent?
Step 3: Find the recurrence — how does dp[i] relate to dp[i-1]?
Step 4: Identify base cases
Step 5: Determine order (top-down memoization OR bottom-up tabulation)

topics.title: 1D DP Problems
subtopics: LC 70: Climbing Stairs — dp[n] = dp[n-1] + dp[n-2]
LC 198: House Robber — dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + nums[i])
LC 322: Coin Change — dp[i] = min(dp[i - coin] + 1) for each coin
LC 300: Longest Increasing Subsequence — dp[i] = max dp[j]+1 where j<i and nums[j]<nums[i]
LC 139: Word Break — dp[i] = any dp[j] where s[j:i] is in wordDict

topics.title: 2D DP Problems
subtopics: LC 62: Unique Paths — dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1]
LC 1143: Longest Common Subsequence — dp[i][j] based on match or no match
LC 518: Coin Change 2 (combinations, not permutations) — 2D DP

questions: What are the two properties a problem must have to be solved with DP?

What is the difference between LC 322 (Coin Change) and LC 518 (Coin Change 2)?
What is the Longest Increasing Subsequence? What is the O(n log n) solution?
How do you identify that a problem has overlapping subproblems?

days.title: Security: Web App Security Every Developer Must Know

topics.title: OWASP Top 10 — Must Know
subtopics: 1. Injection (SQL, NoSQL, Command): parameterized queries, never trust user input
2. Broken Authentication: weak passwords, no MFA, predictable tokens — use bcrypt, JWTs properly
3. XSS (Cross-Site Scripting): reflected, stored, DOM-based. Sanitize output, CSP headers.
4. IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference): check authorization, not just authentication
5. Security Misconfiguration: default credentials, verbose errors, open ports
6. Vulnerable Dependencies: npm audit, dependabot, Snyk
7. Cryptographic Failures: HTTP not HTTPS, weak encryption, storing plaintext passwords

topics.title: Common Attack Vectors & Defenses
subtopics: CSRF: Cross-Site Request Forgery. Defense: CSRF tokens, SameSite cookie attribute.
Clickjacking: X-Frame-Options: DENY header
SQL injection: parameterized queries, ORMs, input validation
Password hashing: bcrypt (cost factor 10-12), argon2id (preferred), NEVER MD5/SHA1 for passwords
Sensitive data: never log passwords/tokens/PII. Mask in logs.

topics.title: HTTPS, TLS, and Certificate Management
subtopics: TLS 1.2 vs 1.3: 1.3 is faster (0-RTT handshake), more secure (removes weak ciphers)
Certificate authorities, self-signed certs (local dev only)
HSTS: force HTTPS even when user types http://
Let's Encrypt: free TLS certs, 90-day rotation, certbot automation

topics.title: Heaps / Priority Queues
subtopics: Python: heapq module — min-heap by default. For max-heap: negate values.
heapq.heappush(heap, item), heapq.heappop(heap) — O(log n)
heapq.nlargest(k, iterable), heapq.nsmallest(k, iterable)

topics.title: Binary Search — Beyond Simple Search
subtopics: Template: lo=0, hi=len-1, while lo<=hi: mid=(lo+hi)//2

LC 33: Search in Rotated Sorted Array — which half is sorted?
LC 153: Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array
LC 875: Koko Eating Bananas — binary search on eating speed (answer)

topics.title: Greedy
subtopics: Greedy: make locally optimal choice at each step, hope it leads to global optimum

LC 455: Assign Cookies, LC 45: Jump Game II, LC 134: Gas Station

subtopics: Hydration mismatch: server renders X, client renders Y → React error. Causes: Date.now(), random(), window checks.
Fix: useEffect for client-only code, suppressHydrationWarning for intentional diffs
Server Component importing Client Component: allowed. Client Component importing Server Component: NOT allowed (use composition).
Large page props: don't pass huge objects as page props — they serialize to JSON and bloat the page
Infinite loop in useEffect: missing deps array, object/array as dep (new reference every render)

questions: What causes memory leaks in Node.js? How do you detect them?
What is a hydration mismatch error? What causes it and how do you fix it?
Can a Client Component import a Server Component? Can a Server Component import a Client Component?
What does --max-old-space-size do for Node.js?
How do you profile a Node.js application in production?
What happens if you run a CPU-intensive task on the Node.js main thread?
How do you fix an infinite loop in useEffect?
Why should you not pass large objects as Next.js page props?
What is V8 garbage collection? What are young and old generation objects?
How does Node.js handle unhandled promise rejections in modern versions (v15+)?

days.title: DSA: Mock Interview x2 + System Design: Autocomplete

mock_sessions.focus: 2 medium problems with a friend or Pramp
Focus on areas you struggled in Mock 1

topics.title: Typeahead/Autocomplete System Design
subtopics: Trie stored in memory, backed by DB for persistence
Top-K suggestions per prefix stored directly in Trie nodes (pre-computed)
Weights: search frequency, recency, personalization
Cache prefix→suggestions in Redis with TTL
Debounce on client: only trigger after 300ms pause in typing
Separate aggregation service: process search logs, update Trie weights offline

days.title: LeetCode Contest + Speed Training
days.tip: Easy LeetCode problems in under 5 minutes signals to interviewers that your fundamentals are solid. Speed matters.

schedule.task: Participate in LeetCode weekly contest (runs every Sunday). Target: solve 2/4 problems in 1.5 hrs.
Speed run — 10 easy problems in 2 hours. Under 5 minutes each.
Review all problems you couldn't solve this week. Understand pattern. Add to Anki or notes.

days.title: FINAL DAY: Retrospective + Launch

transformation.after: Built real-time app with Socket.IO + Redis — proven
Caching patterns, pub/sub, rate limiting — all real
Deep understanding of 4 caching layers, PPR, App Router internals
Multi-stage builds, Compose, healthchecks, CI/CD pipeline
Strict TS, advanced types, proper error typing
SyncSpace (live), portfolio site, technical blog, green GitHub graph
Arrays, linked lists, trees, graphs, DP, heaps, binary search, backtracking
URL shortener, chat, video streaming, Instagram, autocomplete
Ready for 18-30 LPA at product companies

final_checklist: Is SyncSpace live with a URL you can share in every application?
Can you explain the entire Next.js caching architecture from memory?
Can you implement a sliding window rate limiter in Redis without looking it up?
Can you design a chat system end-to-end in 45 minutes on a whiteboard?
Can you solve 2 medium LeetCode problems in 45 minutes under interview conditions?
Is your GitHub contribution graph unbroken for 60 days?
Have you done at least 5 full mock interviews (DSA + system design)?
Is your resume under 1 page with every bullet quantified?
Do you have 3-5 stories in STAR format ready for behavioral questions?
Have you sent at least 30 applications to companies in your target tier?
days.closing_message: The startup shutting down was not the end. It was the beginning of you building the foundation that you should have built before the startup. In 60 days you compressed 2 years of learning that most developers spread across their entire career. Every day you showed up, you became harder to ignore. Every problem you solved, you got closer to the engineer you want to be.
